title: The Annunciation — Tinsae Maryam, The Incarnation of the Word & St. Eustathius of Antioch
narrative:
The Annunciation of the Lord — Tinsae Maryam, the Mystery of the Incarnation of the Eternal Word, and St. Eustathius of Antioch

On the twenty-ninth of Megabit, the holy Church celebrates the most solemn feast in the entire Ethiopian Lenten calendar: the Annunciation of the Lord — Tinsae Maryam in Ge'ez, which means literally the Resurrection of Mary — the day on which the Archangel Gabriel appeared to the Virgin in Nazareth and announced to her the mystery that the entire history of salvation had been preparing since before the foundation of the world. The Ethiopian Church celebrates the Annunciation with the same liturgical solemnity it accords the Nativity and the Resurrection, because in the Ethiopian theological tradition the Incarnation does not begin at Bethlehem but at the moment of Mary's consent in Nazareth: Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word. At that moment the Word became flesh, the eternal Son took to Himself a human nature in the womb of the Virgin, and the great exchange of divinity and humanity that the Nicene and Alexandrian tradition calls the economy of salvation was irrevocably enacted.

The Mystery of the Incarnation of the Eternal Word is commemorated on this day not merely as a historical event but as the ever-present foundation of the entire Christian life — the mystery that gives meaning to the Lenten fast, to the martyrs' deaths, to the monks' desert vigils, and to the ordinary believer's daily prayer. The fast has been a preparation to enter this mystery more deeply; the Lenten weeks of self-emptying have been a participation in the kenosis, the self-emptying of the Word who took on the vulnerability of human flesh. On the day of the Annunciation, the Lenten community stands before the source and summit of everything it has been doing: the divine condescension that makes all human consecration possible, the gift that precedes and enables every response. The Ge'ez liturgical tradition marks this day with some of the most beautiful hymns in the entire Ethiopian liturgical repertoire, hymns addressed to the one who carried the Word in her womb and who, in carrying Him, carried the hope of the entire human race.

Saint Eustathius of Antioch, already commemorated in the Yekatit cycle, returns on the feast of the Annunciation with particular Christological significance: for it was Eustathius who, at Nicaea, most insistently argued for the full reality of Christ's human soul against those who would have dissolved it into the divine nature, and his theological position directly anticipates the Miaphysite tradition's insistence that the Incarnation involves a complete union of complete natures — not a divine person wearing human flesh as a garment but the eternal Word genuinely taking human nature into the most intimate possible union with Himself. On the feast of the Annunciation, when the Church celebrates the moment of that union's beginning, Eustathius's theology stands as the permanent reminder of what the feast is actually affirming.

The Annunciation falls in Megabit, the Lenten month, and this placement is theologically deliberate in the Ethiopian calendar: the feast of the Incarnation belongs in the fast because the Incarnation itself is an act of divine fasting, of the God who holds all things limiting Himself to one woman's womb, one people's history, one man's mortal life. The mystery Mary received when she said yes is the same mystery the Lenten community receives when it says yes to the discipline of the fast: the mystery of becoming a dwelling place for the one who has no need of dwelling places, of emptying oneself so that the fullness might enter. Tinsae Maryam — the Resurrection of Mary — is the name the Ethiopian tradition gives this feast because in receiving the Word into herself, Mary was already risen: the death that sin had brought into human nature was already overcome in the moment of her consent. Amen.
